advantages: Automatically limits training duration when validation performance stops improving — reducing overfitting without changing model architecture.
Saves compute by stopping early instead of running a fixed large epoch budget to completion.
Validation-curve monitoring makes the overfitting signal explicit — train improves while validation stalls or worsens.
Patience provides a tunable buffer against noisy validation metrics on small validation sets.
Restore best weights ensures the deployed checkpoint matches peak validation performance, not the degraded final epoch.
disadvantages: Requires a labeled validation set — unavailable or too small validation data makes curves noisy and stop decisions unreliable.
Patience and min_delta are hyperparameters themselves — wrong values stop too early (underfit) or too late (overfit).
Validation metric choice matters — stopping on val/loss when the business cares about val/f1 can pick a suboptimal checkpoint.
Does not help underfitting — if validation never improves, early stopping only confirms the model or pipeline needs change.
Framework defaults vary — forgetting restore best weights or best_iteration silently deploys worse models despite triggering a stop.
